questions.question: If x, y are two G.M.’s and A is the A.M. between a and b, then A =
questions.question_raw: If x, y are two G.M.’s and A is the A.M. between a and b, then A =
questions.explanation:
Since, x and y are two geometric means between a and b.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a, x, y, b are in G.P.
{tex}\Rightarrow r=\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}a^{\frac{2}{3}} b^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} and y = {tex}a^{\frac{1}{3}} b^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x3 = a2b and y3 = ab2
Now, {tex}\frac{x^{6}}{y^{3}}=\frac{a^{4} b^{2}}{a b^{2}}{/tex} = a3 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{y}{/tex} = a
and {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{y^{6}}=\frac{a^{2} b}{a^{2} b^{4}}=\frac{1}{b^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{y^{2}}=\frac{1}{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b=\frac{y^{2}}{x}{/tex}
Also, A is the A.M. between a and b.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{a+b}{2}=\frac{\frac{x^{2}}{y}+\frac{y^{2}}{x}}{2}=\frac{x^{3}+y^{3}}{2 x y}{/tex}

questions.question: If the product of first five terms of a G.P. is 5^10 and sum of n terms is 3906, then n =
questions.question_raw: If the product of first five terms of a G.P. is 510 and sum of n terms is 3906, then n =
questions.explanation:
Let the terms in G.P. be a, ar, ar2, ar3, ar4, ...
Then, a{tex}\cdot{/tex}ar{tex}\cdot{/tex}ar2{tex}\cdot{/tex}ar3{tex}\cdot{/tex}ar4 = 510
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a5r10 = 510 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar2 = 52
So, it is clear that a = 1 and r = 5 as sum of n terms is rational and not a multiple of 5
Sn = 3906
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1\left(5^{n}-1\right)}{5-1}{/tex} = 3906
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5n = 15625 = 56
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 6

questions.question: If a_1, a_2, ..., a_50 are in G.P., then $\frac{a_{1}-a_{3}+a_{5}-\ldots+a_{49}}{a_{2}-a_{4}+a_{6}-\ldots+a_{50}}$ is:
questions.question_raw: If a1, a2, ..., a50 are in G.P., then {tex}\frac{a_{1}-a_{3}+a_{5}-\ldots+a_{49}}{a_{2}-a_{4}+a_{6}-\ldots+a_{50}}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Let the G.P. be a, ar, ar2, ar3, ..., ar48, ar49
i.e., a1 = a, a2 = ar, a3 = ar2, ..., a49 = ar48 and a50 = ar49
{tex}\therefore \frac{a_{1}-a_{3}+a_{5}-\ldots . .+a_{49}}{a_{2}-a_{4}+a_{6}-\ldots . .+a_{50}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a-a r^{2}+a r^{4}-\ldots . .+a r^{48}}{a r-a r^{3}+a r^{5}-\ldots . .+a r^{49}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{a\left(1-\left(-r^{2}\right)^{25}\right)}{1-\left(-r^{2}\right)}}{\frac{a r\left(1-\left(-r^{2}\right)^{25}\right)}{1-\left(-r^{2}\right)}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{a}{a r}=\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: Let S_k denote the sum of infinite terms of k^th geometric series, where k = 1, 2, ..., 99. If the first term of each series is given by $\frac{k}{(k+1) !}$ and the common ratio is $\frac{1}{k+1}$, then the value of $\frac{(100)^{2}}{100 !}+\sum_\limits{k=1}^{99}$|(k^2 - k - 1) S_k| is:
questions.question_raw: Let Sk denote the sum of infinite terms of kth geometric series, where k = 1, 2, ..., 99. If the first term of each series is given by {tex}\frac{k}{(k+1) !}{/tex} and the common ratio is {tex}\frac{1}{k+1}{/tex}, then the value of {tex}\frac{(100)^{2}}{100 !}+\sum_\limits{k=1}^{99}{/tex}|(k2 - k - 1) Sk| is:
questions.explanation:
Sk = {tex}\frac{\frac{k}{(k+1) !}}{1-\frac{1}{k+1}}=\frac{1}{k !}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sum_\limits{k=1}^{99}{/tex}|(k2 - k - 1) Sk|
= {tex}\sum_\limits{k=1}^{99}\left|\frac{k^{2}-k-1}{k !}\right|=\sum_\limits{k=1}^{99}\left|\frac{k}{(k-1) !}-\frac{k+1}{k !}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{1}{0 !}-\frac{2}{1 !}\right)+\left(\frac{2}{1 !}-\frac{3}{2 !}\right)+\left(\frac{3}{2 !}-\frac{4}{3 !}\right){/tex} + ... + {tex}\left(\frac{99}{98 !}-\frac{100}{99 !}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\left|1-\frac{100}{99 !}\right|=1-\frac{100}{99 !}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required expression = {tex}\frac{(100)^{2}}{100 !}+1-\frac{100}{99 !}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{100}{99 !}+1-\frac{100}{99 !}{/tex}
= 1

questions.question: A G.P. consists of an even number of terms. If the sum of all the terms is 7 times that of the sum of odd terms, then the common ratio is
questions.question_raw: A G.P. consists of an even number of terms. If the sum of all the terms is 7 times that of the sum of odd terms, then the common ratio is
questions.explanation:
Let there be 2n terms in the G.P. with first term a and common ratio r.
S2n = a + ar + ar2 + ... + ar2n-1 = {tex}\frac{a\left(1-r^{2 n}\right)}{1-r}{/tex}
Sum of odd terms = a + ar2 + ar4 + ... + ar2n-2
= a + ar2 + a(r2)2 + ... + a(r2)n-1
= {tex}\frac{a\left[1-\left(r^{2}\right)^{n}\right]}{1-r^{2}}=\frac{a\left(1-r^{2 n}\right)}{1-r^{2}}{/tex}
According to the given condition, {tex}\frac{a\left(1-r^{2 n}\right)}{1-r}=7 \times \frac{a\left(1-r^{2 n}\right)}{1-r^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r + 1 = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 6

questions.question: If a and b are the roots of x^2 - 6x + l = 0, c and d are the roots of x^2 - 24x + m = 0, where a, b, c, d are in G.P., then (m + l) : (m - l) =
questions.question_raw: If a and b are the roots of x2 - 6x + l = 0, c and d are the roots of x2 - 24x + m = 0, where a, b, c, d are in G.P., then (m + l) : (m - l) =
questions.explanation:
Given, a, b, c, d are in G.P. Let a = {tex}\frac {k}{r^{3}}{/tex}, b = {tex}\frac {k}r{/tex}, c = kr and d = kr3
From the given quadratic equations, a + b = 6 and c + d = 24
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k}{r^{3}}+\frac{k}{r}{/tex} = 6 and kr + kr3 = 24
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k}{r^{3}}{/tex}(1 + r2) = 6 and kr(1 + r2) = 24
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{k r\left(1+r^{2}\right)}{\frac{k}{r^{3}}\left(1+r^{2}\right)}=\frac{24}{6} \Rightarrow{/tex} r4 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
Also, l = ab = {tex}\frac{k}{(\sqrt{2})^{3}} \times \frac{k}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{k^{2}}{4}{/tex}
and m = cd = [k {tex}\cdot \sqrt{2}{/tex}] {tex}\times\left[k(\sqrt{2})^{3}\right]{/tex} = 4k2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (m + l) : (m - l) = {tex}\left(4 k^{2}+\frac{k^{2}}{4}\right):\left(4 k^{2}-\frac{k^{2}}{4}\right){/tex}
= 17: 15

questions.question: If 1 + cos $\alpha$ + cos^2 $\alpha$ + ... $\infty$ = 2 - $\sqrt 2$, then $\alpha$, (0 < $\alpha$ < $\pi$) is:
questions.question_raw: If 1 + cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} + cos2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + ... {tex}\infty{/tex} = 2 - {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex}, then {tex}\alpha{/tex}, (0 < {tex}\alpha{/tex} < {tex}\pi{/tex}) is:
questions.explanation:
We have,
1 + cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} + cos2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + ... infinite terms = 2 - {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1-\cos \alpha}{/tex} = 2 - {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} ...[{tex}\because{/tex} a = 1 and r = cos {tex}\alpha{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - cos {tex}\alpha=\frac{1}{2-\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - cos {tex}\alpha=\frac{2+\sqrt{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - cos {tex}\alpha=1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\alpha=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\cos \frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha =\frac {3\pi}4{/tex}

questions.question: If A = 1 + r^2 + r^2z + r^3z + ... infinite terms, then the value of r will be

questions.question: If in an infinite G.P. first term is equal to twice of the sum of the remaining terms, then its common ratio is:

questions.question: Sum of infinite number of terms in G.P. is 20 and sum of their squares is 100. The common ratio of G.P. is:
questions.question_raw: Sum of infinite number of terms in G.P. is 20 and sum of their squares is 100. The common ratio of G.P. is:

questions.question: Let S_0 be a unit square, S_k be the square formed by joining the midpoints of the sides of S_k-1, k = 1, 2, 3, ... If l_n and A_n represent the length of side and area respectively of square S_n, then which of the following is not correct?
questions.question_raw: Let S0 be a unit square, Sk be the square formed by joining the midpoints of the sides of Sk-1, k = 1, 2, 3, ... If ln and An represent the length of side and area respectively of square Sn, then which of the following is not correct?

options.text: Sum of perimeters of all the squares is 4$\sqrt 2$ ($\sqrt 2$ + 1)

questions.explanation:
l0 = 1, l1 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}, l2 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}, ... form a G.P. with common ratio {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
A0 = 1, A1 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}, A2 = {tex}\frac 14{/tex}, ... form a G.P. with common ratio {tex}\frac 12{/tex}
{tex}\frac{l_{n}}{l_{n+1}}=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} : 1, where {tex}\frac{l_{n}}{l_{n+1}}{/tex} is the reciprocal of the common ratio.
{tex}\sum_\limits{i=0}^{\infty} \mathrm{A}_{i}=\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = 2
Sum of perimeters of all the squares
= 4(1 + {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + ...) = 4 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}\right)=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{4 \sqrt{2}(\sqrt{2}+1)}{2-1}{/tex} = 4{tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} ({tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} + 1)
(An+1)2 : (An)2 = {tex}\frac 14{/tex} : 1 = 1 : 4, where {tex}\frac{A_{n+1}}{A_{n}}{/tex} is the common ratio.

questions.question: The product (32)$(32)^{\frac{1}{6}}(32)^{\frac{1}{36}}$ ... to $\infty$ is:

(32){tex}(32)^{\frac{1}{6}}(32)^{\frac{1}{36}}{/tex} ... = {tex}(32)^{1+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{36}+-}{/tex}

questions.question: If a, a_1, a_2, a_3, ..., a_2n, b are in A.P.; a, g_1, g_2, ..., g_2n, b are in G.P. and h is the harmonic mean of a, b, then $\frac{a_{1}+a_{2 n}}{g_{1} g_{2 n}}+\frac{a_{2}+a_{2 n-1}}{g_{2} g_{2 n-1}}$ + ... + $\frac{a_{n}+a_{n+1}}{g_{n} g_{n+1}}$ =
questions.question_raw: If a, a1, a2, a3, ..., a2n, b are in A.P.; a, g1, g2, ..., g2n, b are in G.P. and h is the harmonic mean of a, b, then {tex}\frac{a_{1}+a_{2 n}}{g_{1} g_{2 n}}+\frac{a_{2}+a_{2 n-1}}{g_{2} g_{2 n-1}}{/tex} + ... + {tex}\frac{a_{n}+a_{n+1}}{g_{n} g_{n+1}}{/tex} =

a1 + a2n = a2 + a2n-1 = ... = an + an+1 = a + b
g1 g2n = g2g2n-1 = ... = gngn-1 = ab
h is the harmonic mean of a and b.
{tex}\Leftrightarrow h=\frac{2 a b}{a+b}{/tex} ...(i)

= {tex}n\left(\frac{a+b}{a b}\right)=\frac{2 n}{h}{/tex} ...[From (i)]

questions.question: If a > 0, then $\sum_\limits{n=1}^{\infty}\left(\frac{a}{a+1}\right)^{n}$ equals:
questions.question_raw: If a > 0, then {tex}\sum_\limits{n=1}^{\infty}\left(\frac{a}{a+1}\right)^{n}{/tex} equals:
questions.explanation:
We have,
{tex}\sum_\limits{n=1}^{\infty}\left(\frac{a}{a+1}\right)^{n}=\frac{a}{a+1}+\left(\frac{a}{a+1}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{a}{a+1}\right)^{3}+\ldots{/tex}
Here, first term = {tex}\frac{a}{a+1}{/tex}
and common ratio = {tex}\frac{a}{a+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sum_\limits{n=1}^{\infty}\left(\frac{a}{a+1}\right)^{n}=\frac{\frac{a}{a+1}}{1-\frac{a}{a+1}}{/tex} = a

questions.question: The sum of infinite terms of the geometric progression $\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}, \frac{1}{2-\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{2}$ ... is:
questions.question_raw: The sum of infinite terms of the geometric progression {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}, \frac{1}{2-\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{2}{/tex} ... is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{2}-1)}, \frac{1}{2}, \ldots{/tex}
Common ratio of the series = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{2}+1)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum = {tex}\frac{a}{1-r}=\frac {\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}\right)}{\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{2}+1)}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{2}+1)}{(\sqrt{2}-1)} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{2}+1)}{(1+\sqrt{2})}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}({tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} + 1)2

questions.question: The value of n for which $\frac{x^{n+1}+y^{n+1}}{x^{n}+y^{n}}$ is the geometric mean of x and y is:
questions.question_raw: The value of n for which {tex}\frac{x^{n+1}+y^{n+1}}{x^{n}+y^{n}}{/tex} is the geometric mean of x and y is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{x^{n+1}+y^{n+1}}{x^{n}+y^{n}}=\sqrt{x y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xn+1 + yn+1 = {tex}\sqrt{x y}{/tex}(xn + yn)
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{n+\frac{1}{2}}\left(x^{\frac{1}{2}}-y^{\frac{1}{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}y^{n+\frac{1}{2}}\left(x^{\frac{1}{2}}-y^{\frac{1}{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)^{n+\frac{1}{2}}=1 \Rightarrow{/tex} n = -{tex}\frac 12{/tex}

questions.question: The two geometric means between the numbers 1 and 64 are:

questions.question: If a, b, c are in G.P. and log a - log 2b, log 2b - log 3c and log 3c - log a are in A.P., then a, b, c are the lengths of the sides of a triangle which is:
questions.question_raw: If a, b, c are in G.P. and log a - log 2b, log 2b - log 3c and log 3c - log a are in A.P., then a, b, c are the lengths of the sides of a triangle which is:
questions.explanation:
According to the given conditions,
b2 = ac and 2(log2b - log3c) = loga - log2b + log3c - loga
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 log {tex}\left(\frac{2 b}{3 c}\right){/tex} = log {tex}\left(\frac{3 c}{2 b}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log{tex}\left(\frac{2 b}{3 c}\right)^2{/tex} = log {tex}\left(\frac{3 c}{2 b}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{b}{c}\right)^{3}=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{b}{c}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Now, b2 = ac and 2b = 3c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = {tex}\frac{2 a}{3}{/tex} and c = {tex}\frac{4 a}{9}{/tex}
Since, a + b = {tex}\frac{5 a}{3}{/tex} > c, b + c = {tex}\frac{10 a}{9}{/tex} > a, c + a = {tex}\frac{13 a}{9}{/tex} > b
It implies that a, b, c form a triangle with a as the greatest side.
Let us find the greatest angle A of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC by using the cosine formula.
cos A = {tex}\frac{b^{2}+c^{2}-a^{2}}{2 b c}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{29}{48}{/tex} < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The angle A is obtuse.

questions.question: If five G.M.s are inserted between 486 and $\frac 23$, then fourth G.M. equals

Then, total terms are 7.
Now, Tn = arn-1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{3}{/tex} = 486 (r)6 {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4th G.M. is,
T5 = ar4 = 486{tex}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{4}{/tex} = 6

questions.question: If for 0 < x < $\frac {\pi}2$, y = exp [(sin^2 x + sin^4 x + sin^6 x + ...$\infty$) log_e 2] is a root of the quadratic equation x^2 - 9x + 8 = 0, then the value of $\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sin x-\cos x}$ is:
questions.question_raw: If for 0 < x < {tex}\frac {\pi}2{/tex}, y = exp [(sin2 x + sin4 x + sin6 x + ...{tex}\infty{/tex}) loge 2] is a root of the quadratic equation x2 - 9x + 8 = 0, then the value of {tex}\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sin x-\cos x}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
For 0 < x < {tex}\frac {\pi}2{/tex}, we have 0 < sin2 x < 1.
Now,
y = exp [(sin2x + sin4x + sin6x +...) loge 2]
= exp {tex}\left[\left(\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{1-\sin ^{2} x}\right) \log _{e} 2\right]{/tex} ...[{tex}\because{/tex} S = {tex}\frac{a}{1-r}{/tex}]
= exp (tan2 x loge 2)
= exp [loge{tex}(2)^{\tan^2 x}{/tex}] = {tex}e^{\log_e(2)^{\tan^2x}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = {tex}2^{\tan^2 x}{/tex} ...(i)
Since y satisfies the equation x2 - 9x + 8 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y2 - 9y + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 1)(y - 8) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1 or y = 8
For y = 1, {tex}2^{\tan^2 x}{/tex} = 1 ...[From (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2^{\tan^2 x}{/tex} = 20
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0
But 0 < x < {tex}\frac {\pi}2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
For y = 8,
{tex}2^{\tan^2 x}{/tex} = 23 ...[From (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan2 x = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sin x-\cos x}=\frac{\tan x+1}{\tan x-1}=\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2}}{(\sqrt{3}-1)(\sqrt{3}+1)}{/tex}
= 2 + {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}

questions.question: The G.M. of the numbers 3, 3^2, 3^3, ..., 3^n is:
questions.question_raw: The G.M. of the numbers 3, 32, 33, ..., 3n is:

G.M. = {tex}\left(3.3^{2} \cdot 3^{3} \ldots 3^{n}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}{/tex}

questions.question: The A.M. and G.M. of two numbers are 91 and 84 respectively. The two numbers are:
questions.question_raw: The A.M. and G.M. of two numbers are 91 and 84 respectively. The two numbers are:
questions.explanation:
Let the two numbers be x and y. Then,
{tex}\frac{x+y}{2}{/tex} = 91 and {tex}\sqrt{x y}{/tex} = 84
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y = 182 ...(i)
and xy = 7056
x - y = {tex}\sqrt{(x+y)^{2}-4 x y}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{182^{2}-4(7056)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x - y = 70 ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
x = 126 and y = 56

questions.question: If A is the A.M. of the roots of the equation x^2 - 2ax + b^2 = 0 and G is the G.M. of the roots of the equation x^2 - 2bx + a^2 = 0, then:
questions.question_raw: If A is the A.M. of the roots of the equation x2 - 2ax + b2 = 0 and G is the G.M. of the roots of the equation x2 - 2bx + a2 = 0, then:
questions.explanation:
Sum of the roots of x2 - 2ax + b2 = 0 is 2a.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = A.M. of the roots = a
Product of the roots of x2 - 2bx + a2 = 0 is a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} G.M. of the roots is G = a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A = G

questions.question: Let S_1, S_2, ... be squares such that for each n $\geq$ 1, the length of a side of S_n equals the length of a diagonal of S_n+1. If the length of a side of S_1 is 10 cm, then for which of the following values of n is the area of S_n less than 1 sq. cm?
questions.question_raw: Let S1, S2, ... be squares such that for each n {tex}\geq{/tex} 1, the length of a side of Sn equals the length of a diagonal of Sn+1. If the length of a side of S1 is 10 cm, then for which of the following values of n is the area of Sn less than 1 sq. cm?
questions.explanation:
We have,
Length of a side of Sn
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Length of a diagonal of Sn+1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Length of a side of Sn
= {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} (Length of a side of Sn+1)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\text { Length of a sideof } S_{n+1}}{\text { Length of a side of } S_{n}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} for all n {tex}\geq{/tex} 1
Sides of S1, S2, ..., Sn form a G.P. with common ratio {tex}\frac 1{\sqrt 2}{/tex} and first term 10.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} length of the side of Sn = 10{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{n-1}=\frac{10}{2^{\frac{n-1}{2}}}{/tex}
Now, area of Sn = (side)2 = {tex}\left(\frac{10}{2^{\frac{n-1}{2}}}\right)^{2}=\frac{100}{2^{n-1}}{/tex}
But, area of Sn < 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{100}{2^{n-1}}{/tex} < 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2n-1 > 100
The above inequality is satisfied if n - 1 {tex}\geq{/tex} 7 i.e. if n {tex}\geq{/tex} 8

questions.question: The A.M. and G.M. of roots of a quadratic equation are 30 and 18 respectively, then the ratio of roots is:
questions.question_raw: The A.M. and G.M. of roots of a quadratic equation are 30 and 18 respectively, then the ratio of roots is:
questions.explanation:
Let the roots be {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} Then,
{tex}\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}{/tex} = 30 {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta{/tex} = 60
and {tex}\sqrt{\alpha \beta}{/tex} = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha \beta{/tex} = 324
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The quadratic equation is x2 - 60x + 324 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 54)(x - 6) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 54, 6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of roots = 54 : 6 = 9 : 1

questions.question: Two sequences {t_n} and {S_n} are defined by t_n = log$\left(\frac{5^{n+1}}{3^{n-1}}\right)$, S_n = $\left[\log \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)\right]^{n}$, then:

options.text: {S_n} is a G.P., {t_n} is neither A.P. nor G.P.
{t_n} is an A.P., {S_n} is a G.P.

questions.explanation:
tn = log{tex}\left(\frac{5^{n+1}}{3^{n-1}}\right){/tex}; Sn = {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)\right]^{n}{/tex}
t1 = log 25; S1 = {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)\right]^1{/tex}
t2 = log {tex}\frac {125}3{/tex}; S2 = {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)\right]^2{/tex}

Clearly, tn is an A.P. and Sn is a G.P.

questions.question: If A_1, A_2; G_1, G_2 and H_1, H_2 are two A.M.’s, G.M.’s and H.M.’s between two numbers respectively, then $\frac{{G}_{1} G_{2}}{H_{1} H_{2}} \times \frac{H_{1}+H_{2}}{A_{1}+A_{2}}$ =
questions.question_raw: If A1, A2; G1, G2 and H1, H2 are two A.M.’s, G.M.’s and H.M.’s between two numbers respectively, then {tex}\frac{{G}_{1} G_{2}}{H_{1} H_{2}} \times \frac{H_{1}+H_{2}}{A_{1}+A_{2}}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Let a and b be two numbers.
Sum of n A.M.'s = n {tex}\times{/tex} A.M. of a and b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A1 + A2 = 2 {tex}\times\left(\frac{a+b}{2}\right){/tex} = a + b
Product of n G.M.'s = (G.M. of a and b)n
{tex}\Rightarrow G_{1} \cdot G_{2}=(\sqrt{a b})^{2}{/tex} = ab
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{H_{1}}, \frac{1}{H_{2}}, \frac{1}{b}{/tex} are in A.P.
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{H_{1}}+\frac{1}{H_{2}}=\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}=\frac{a+b}{a b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{H_{1} H_{2}}{H_{1}+H_{2}} = \frac{G_{1} G_{2}}{A_{1}+A_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{G_{1} G_{2}}{H_{1} H_{2}} \times \frac{H_{1}+H_{2}}{A_{1}+A_{2}}{/tex} = 1

questions.question: If tan $\theta$ + $\frac{\tan \theta}{2}+\frac{\tan \theta}{4}+\ldots \infty$ = 1 + log_2 $\sqrt 6$ + log_2 $\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{\frac{-1}{2}}$, then the values of $\theta$ are in
questions.question_raw: If tan {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\frac{\tan \theta}{2}+\frac{\tan \theta}{4}+\ldots \infty{/tex} = 1 + log2 {tex}\sqrt 6{/tex} + log2 {tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{\frac{-1}{2}}{/tex}, then the values of {tex}\theta{/tex} are in
questions.explanation:
tan {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\frac{\tan \theta}{2}+\frac{\tan \theta}{4}+\ldots \infty{/tex} = 1 + log2 {tex}\sqrt 6{/tex} + log2 {tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{\frac{-1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan {tex}\theta\left(1+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2^{2}}+\ldots\right){/tex} = 1 + log2 {tex}\sqrt 6{/tex} + log2 {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan {tex}\theta\left(\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{2}}\right){/tex} = 1 + log2{tex}\left(\sqrt{6} \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 + log2 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 + 1 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 = tan {tex}\frac {\pi}4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = n{tex}\pi + \frac {\pi}4{/tex}, n {tex}\in{/tex} Z
The values of {tex}\theta{/tex} obtained by putting different values of n will be in A.P.

questions.question: The sum of a G.P. with common ratio 3 is 364, and last term is 243, then the number of terms is:
questions.question_raw: The sum of a G.P. with common ratio 3 is 364, and last term is 243, then the number of terms is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{a r^{n}-a}{r-1}{/tex} = 364 ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a r^{n-1} \cdot r-a}{r-1}{/tex} = 364
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3 \times 243-a}{2}{/tex} = 364
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1
Putting a = 1 in (i), we get n = 6

questions.question: The sum of n terms of the series 0.8 + 0.88 + 0.888 + ... is:
questions.question_raw: The sum of n terms of the series 0.8 + 0.88 + 0.888 + ... is:
questions.explanation:
0.8 + 0.88 + 0.888 + ... upto n terms
= 8(0.1 + 0.11 + 0.111 + .. .upto n terms)
= {tex}\frac 89{/tex}(0.9 + 0.99 + 0.999 + ... upto n terms)
= {tex}\frac 89{/tex}({tex}\frac{9}{10}+\frac{99}{100}+\frac{999}{1000}{/tex}+ ... upto n terms)
= {tex}\frac 89{/tex}({tex}\left(1-\frac{1}{10}\right)+\left(1-\frac{1}{100}\right){/tex} + ... upto n terms)
= {tex}\frac 89{/tex}((1 + 1 + 1 + ... n terms) - ({tex}\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{10^{2}}+\frac{1}{10^{3}}{/tex} + ... upto n terms)]
= {tex}\frac{8}{9}\left[n-\frac{\frac{1}{10}\left(1-\frac{1}{10^{n}}\right)}{1-\frac{1}{10}}\right]{/tex}= {tex}\frac{8}{81}\left[9 n-1+\left(\frac{1}{10^{n}}\right)\right] {/tex}

questions.question: $\sum_\limits{r=1}^{10} \frac{r}{(r+1)(r+2)}$ (2^r-1) =
questions.question_raw: {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{10} \frac{r}{(r+1)(r+2)}{/tex} (2r-1) =
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{10} \frac{r}{(r+1)(r+2)}{/tex} (2r-1) = {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{10} 2^{r-1}\left[\frac{r+1-1}{(r+1)(r+2)}\right] {/tex}
= {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{10} 2^{r-1}\left[\frac{1}{r+2}-\frac{r+2-(r+1)}{(r+1)(r+2)}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{10} 2^{r-1}\left[\frac{2}{r+2}-\frac{1}{(r+1)}\right] {/tex}
= {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{10}\left(\frac{2^{r}}{r+2}-\frac{2^{r-1}}{r+1}\right)=\frac{2^{10}}{10+2}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1024}{12}-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{256}{3}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {509}6{/tex}

questions.question: The value of $(0.5)^{\log_{\sqrt 2(\frac 1{10}+\frac 1{20}+\frac 1{40}+...\infty)}}$ is:
questions.question_raw: The value of {tex}(0.5)^{\log_{\sqrt 2(\frac 1{10}+\frac 1{20}+\frac 1{40}+...\infty)}}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
Let y = {tex}(0.5)^{\log_{\sqrt 2(\frac 1{10}+\frac 1{20}+\frac 1{40}+...\infty)}}{/tex} = {tex}(0.5)^{\log _{\sqrt{2}}}\left|\frac{\frac{1}{10}}{1-\frac{1}{2}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}(0.5)^{\log _{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} log y = {tex}\log _{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{1}{5}\right) \cdot{/tex} log (0.5)
= {tex}\left(-\log _{\sqrt{2}} 5\right) \log \left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\log 5 \cdot \log 2}{\frac{1}{2} \log 2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = 2 log 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 52 = 25

questions.question: $0.4\overset \bullet 2\overset \bullet3$ =
questions.question_raw: {tex}0.4\overset \bullet 2\overset \bullet3{/tex} =

{tex}0.4\overset \bullet 2\overset \bullet3{/tex} = 0.4232323 ...
= 0.4 + 0.023 + 0.00023 + 0.0000023 + ...

questions.question: If the ratio of H.M. and G.M. between two numbers a and b is 4:5, then the ratio of the two numbers is:
questions.question_raw: If the ratio of H.M. and G.M. between two numbers a and b is 4:5, then the ratio of the two numbers is:
questions.explanation:
We have H.M. = {tex}\frac{2 a b}{a+b}{/tex} and G.M. = {tex}\sqrt{a b}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\text { H.M. }}{\text { G.M. }}=\frac{4}{5} \Rightarrow \frac{\frac {2ab}{(a+b)}}{\sqrt{a b}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \sqrt{a b}}{(a+b)}=\frac{4}{5} \Rightarrow \frac{a+b}{2 \sqrt{a b}}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a+b+2 \sqrt{a b}}{a+b-2 \sqrt{a b}}=\frac{5+4}{5-4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})^{2}}{(\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b})^{2}}=\frac{9}{1} \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b}}{\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b}}=\frac{3}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})+(\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b})}{(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})-(\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b})}=\frac{3+1}{3-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \sqrt{a}}{2 \sqrt{b}}=\frac{4}{2} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{a}{b}\right){/tex} = 22 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  a:b = 4:1 or b:a = 1:4

questions.question: The sum $\sum \limits_{\mathrm n=1}^\infty\\$$\frac{2n^2+3n+4}{(2n)!}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The sum {tex}\sum \limits_{\mathrm n=1}^\infty\\{/tex}{tex}\frac{2n^2+3n+4}{(2n)!}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}\sum \limits_{\mathrm n=1}^\infty\\{/tex}{tex}\frac{2n^2+3n+4}{(2n)!}{/tex}
Multiply and divide by 2.
={tex}\frac12{/tex}{tex}\sum \limits_{\mathrm n=1}^\infty\\{/tex}{tex}\frac{2n(2n-1)+8n+8}{(2n)!}{/tex}
={tex}=\frac{1}{2} \sum \limits_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2 n-2) !}+2 \sum \limits_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2 n-1) !}+4 \sum \limits_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2 n) !}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, apply exponential series with x = 1
e = 1 + 1 + {tex}\frac{1}{2!}+\frac{1}{3!}+\frac{1}{4!}{/tex} +...
Now, put x = -1 in exponential series
e-1 = 1 - 1 + {tex}\frac{1}{2!}+\frac{1}{3!}+\frac{1}{4!}{/tex} +...
{tex}\left(\mathrm e+\frac1{\mathrm e}\right){/tex} = 2{tex}\left(1+\frac1{2!}+\frac1{4!}+...\right){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm e-\frac1{\mathrm e}=\left(1+\frac1{3!}+\frac1{5!}+...\right){/tex} ...(ii)
Now, from (i),
{tex}\frac12\left(\sum_{\mathrm n=1}^\infty\frac1{(2\mathrm n-2)!}\right)+2\sum_{\mathrm n=1}^\infty\frac1{(2\mathrm n-1)!}+4\sum_{\mathrm n=1}^\infty\frac1{(2\mathrm n)!}\\\;{/tex}
Now, use equation (ii),
{tex}=\frac12\left[\frac{\mathrm e+{\displaystyle\frac1{\mathrm e}}}2\right]+\left[\frac{\mathrm e-{\displaystyle\frac1{\mathrm e}}}2\right]+4\left[\frac{\mathrm e+{\displaystyle\frac1{\mathrm e}}-2}2\right]\\\;{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\left(\mathrm e+{\displaystyle\frac1{\mathrm e}}\right)}4+\mathrm e-\frac1{\mathrm e}+2\mathrm e+\frac2{\mathrm e}-4{/tex} {tex}=\frac{13}4\mathrm e+\frac5{4\mathrm e}-4{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(r^{\frac {-3}{ 2}}+r^{\frac 3 2}\right)+\left(r^{\frac 1 2}+r^{\frac {-1}{ 2}}\right)=21{/tex}

questions.question: Consider two G.Ps. 2, 2^2, 2^3, ... and 4, 4^2, 4^3,... of 60 and n terms respectively. If the geometric mean of all the 60 + n terms is (2)^$\frac {225} {8}$, then $\sum \limits_{k=1}^n k(n-k)$ is equal to:

Compare powers,
{tex}\frac{60 \times \frac{61}{2}+n(n+1)}{60+n}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{225}{8}{/tex}
Denominator (60 + n) should be multiple of 8. Then, put n = 20.

= 20{tex}\left(\frac{20 \times 21}{2}\right)-\frac{20 \times(21) \times 41}{6}{/tex} = 4200 - 2870 = 1330

B ={tex}\left(\frac{-1}{2}+\frac{-1}{2^3}+\ldots \infty\right)+\left(\frac{1}{4^2}+\frac{1}{4^4}+\ldots \infty\right){/tex}
B = {tex}\frac{-\frac{1}{2}}{1-\frac{1}{4}}+\frac{\frac{1}{16}}{1-\frac{1}{16}} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow B=-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{3}+\frac{1}{16} \times \frac{16}{15}{/tex}
B = -{tex}\frac{9}{15} ; \frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{11}{15} \times \frac{15}{(-9)} ; \frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{B}}=-\frac{11}{9}{/tex}

questions.question: If $ \frac{1}{2 \cdot 3^{10}}+\frac{1}{2^2 \cdot 3^9}+\cdots \frac{1}{2^{10} \cdot 3}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{2^{10} \cdot 3^{10}}$, then the remainder when K is divided by 6 is
questions.question_raw: If {tex} \frac{1}{2 \cdot 3^{10}}+\frac{1}{2^2 \cdot 3^9}+\cdots \frac{1}{2^{10} \cdot 3}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{2^{10} \cdot 3^{10}}{/tex}, then the remainder when K is divided by 6 is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{1}{2.3^{10}}+\frac{1}{2^2 \cdot 3^9}+\frac{1}{2^3 \cdot 3^8}{/tex}{tex}+\ldots .+\frac{1}{2^{10} \cdot 3}=\frac{K}{2^{10} \cdot 3^{10}}{/tex}
K = 29 + 28 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 3 + 27 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 32 +... + 39
Series K forms a GP. with first term, a = 29
and {tex}r=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)=\frac{2^9\left(\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{10}-1\right)}{\frac{3}{2}-1}{/tex} = 310 - 210
Take, 310 - 210 = ((35)2 - (25)2) = (35 - 25) (35 + 25)
= (211) (275) = (35 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 + 1) (45 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 + 5) = 6{tex}\mu{/tex} + 5
Therefore, remainder is 5.

questions.question: If the sum of an infinite GP a, ar, ar^2, ar^3,... is 15 and the sum of the squares of its each term is 150, then the sum of ar^2, ar^4, ar^6,... is:
questions.question_raw: If the sum of an infinite GP a, ar, ar2, ar3,... is 15 and the sum of the squares of its each term is 150, then the sum of ar2, ar4, ar6,... is:
questions.explanation:
Sum of infinite terms of series a + ar + ar2 +... = 15
{tex}\therefore \frac{a}{1-r}=15{/tex} ... (i)
Sequence formed by square of terms:
a2, a2, r2, a2, r4, a2, r6...
a2 + a2r2 +... = 150
Sum = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{1-\mathrm{r}^2}{/tex} = 150
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a}{1-r} \cdot \frac{a}{1+r}=15 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 15 \cdot \frac{a}{1+r}=150{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \frac{a}{1-r}=15\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a}{1+r}=10{/tex} ...(ii)
On dividing equation (l) by (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+r}{1-r}=\frac{15}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{1}{5} \Rightarrow{/tex} a = 12
Now series: ar2 + ar4 + ar6 +...
Sum = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{ar}^2}{1-\mathrm{r}^2}=\frac{12 \cdot\left(\frac{1}{25}\right)}{1-\frac{1}{25}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: In an increasing geometric series, the sum of the second $\frac {25}{7}$ and the sixth term is $\frac {25}2$ and the product of the third and fifth term is 25. Then, the sum of 4^th, 6^th and 8^th term is equal to:
questions.question_raw: In an increasing geometric series, the sum of the second {tex}\frac {25}{7}{/tex} and the sixth term is {tex}\frac {25}2{/tex} and the product of the third and fifth term is 25. Then, the sum of 4th, 6th and 8th term is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let first term and common ratio are a and r respectively. Then,
a2 + a6 = {tex}\frac {25}2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar + ar5 = {tex}\frac {25}2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar(1 + r4) = {tex}\frac {25}2{/tex} ...(i)
and a3 {tex}\times{/tex} a5 = 25 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2r6 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ar3 = 5 ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2r4 - 5r2 + 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} and r2 = 2
{tex}\because{/tex} GP is increasing so take r2 = 2
Now, a4 + a6 + a8 = ar3 (1 + r2 + r4) = 35

questions.question: Let a, b, c, d and p be any non zero distinct real numbers such that (a^2 + b^2 + c^2)p^2 - 2(ab + bc + cd)p + (b^2 + c^2 + d^2) = 0. Then:
questions.question_raw: Let a, b, c, d and p be any non zero distinct real numbers such that (a2 + b2 + c2)p2 - 2(ab + bc + cd)p + (b2 + c2 + d2) = 0. Then:

options.text: a, c, p are in G.P.
a, b, c, d are in A.P.
a, b, c, d are in G.P.
a, c, p are in A.P.

questions.explanation:
Rearrange given equation, we get
(a2p2 - 2abp + b2) + (b2p2 - 2bcp + c2) + (c2p2 - 2cdp + d2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (ap - b)2 + (bp - c)2 + (cp - d)2 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ap - b = bp - c = cp - d = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{b}{a}=\frac{c}{b}=\frac{d}{c}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} a, b, c, d are in GP.

questions.question: Suppose a, b, c are in A.P. and a^2, b^2, c^2 are in GP. 3 if a < b < c and a + b + c = $\frac 32$, then the value of a is
questions.question_raw: Suppose a, b, c are in A.P. and a2, b2, c2 are in GP. 3 if a < b < c and a + b + c = {tex}\frac 32{/tex}, then the value of a is
questions.explanation:
Since a, b, c are in A.P.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2b = a + c
But given a + b + c = {tex}\frac 32{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} and then a + c = 1
Also a2, b2, c2, are in GP. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b4 = a2 c2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = {tex}\pm{/tex}ac {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ac = {tex}\frac 14{/tex}or -{tex}\frac 14{/tex} and a + c = 1
Considering a + c = 1 and ac = {tex}\frac 14{/tex} ...(i)
(a - c)2 = 1 - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = c but a {tex}\neq{/tex} c as given that a < b < c
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a + c = 1 and ac = {tex}-\frac 14{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a - c)2 = 1 + 1 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - c = {tex}\pm \sqrt{2}{/tex}
but a < c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - c = -{tex}\sqrt2{/tex} ...(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get a {tex}=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: Let A_1 and A_2 be two arithmetic means and G_1, G_2, G_3 be three geometric means of two distinct positive numbers. The $\mathrm G_1^4+\mathrm G_2^4+\mathrm G_3^4+\mathrm G_1^2\;\mathrm G_3^2$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let A1 and A2 be two arithmetic means and G1, G2, G3 be three geometric means of two distinct positive numbers. The {tex}\mathrm G_1^4+\mathrm G_2^4+\mathrm G_3^4+\mathrm G_1^2\;\mathrm G_3^2{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Since, a, A1, A2, b are in A.P.
A1 = {tex}\frac{20+b}{3}{/tex}; A2 = {tex}\frac{a+2b}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A1 + A2 = a + b
a, G1, G2, G3, b are in G.P.
{tex}G_1=\left(a^3 b\right)^{\frac{1}{4}} {/tex}; {tex}\mathrm{G}_2=\left(\mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{~b}^2\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}, {tex}\mathrm{G}_3=\left(a \mathrm{~b}^3\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\mathrm G_1^4+\mathrm G_2^4+\mathrm G_3^4+\mathrm G_1^2\;\mathrm G_3^2{/tex} = {tex}a^3 b+a^2 b^2+a b^3+\left(a^3 b\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot\left(a b^3\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
= a3b + a2b2 + ab3 +a2{tex}\cdot{/tex}b2 = ab(a2 + 2ab + b2)
= ab(a + b)2
= G1{tex}\cdot{/tex}G3{tex}\cdot{/tex}(A1 A2)2

questions.question: Let a, b, c and d be positive real numbers such that a + b + c + d = 11. If the maximum value of a^5b^3c^2d is 3750$\beta$, then the value of $\beta$ is
questions.question_raw: Let a, b, c and d be positive real numbers such that a + b + c + d = 11. If the maximum value of a5b3c2d is 3750{tex}\beta{/tex}, then the value of {tex}\beta{/tex} is

A.M. {tex}\geq{/tex} G.M.

{tex}1>\left(\frac{a^5 b^3 c^2 d}{5^5 3^3 2^2}\right)^{1 / 11}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 5^5 3^3 2^2 \geq 3750 \beta{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \beta=90^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.question: If the minimum value of $f(x)=\frac{5 x^2}{2}+\frac{\alpha}{x^5}, x>0$, is 14, then the value of $\alpha$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the minimum value of {tex}f(x)=\frac{5 x^2}{2}+\frac{\alpha}{x^5}, x>0{/tex}, is 14, then the value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
We can write f(x) is in the form of
{tex}\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^2}{2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{\alpha}{2 x^5}+\frac{\alpha}{2 x^5} \geq 7\left(\frac{\alpha^2}{2^7}\right)^{\frac{1}{7}}{/tex}
Now {tex}\frac{7 \cdot(\alpha)^{2 / 7}}{2}=14{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\alpha^2\right)^{1 / 7}=2^2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\alpha=\left(2^2\right)^{7 / 2}=2^7{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 128

questions.question: Let x, y > 0. If x^3y^2 = 2^15, then the least value of 3x + 2y is
questions.question_raw: Let x, y > 0. If x3y2 = 215, then the least value of 3x + 2y is
questions.explanation:
Given expression is x3y2 = 215
So, there are 3 x’s and 2 y’s.
Using AM {tex}\geq{/tex} GM,
{tex}\frac{x+x+x+y+y}{5} \geq\left(x^3 \cdot y^2\right)^{\frac{1}{5}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 x+2 y}{5} \geq\left(2^{15}\right)^{\frac{1}{5}}=2^3{/tex}
Therefore, the least value is 40.

questions.question: If x > 1, y > 1, z > 1 are in GP., then $\frac{1}{1+\ln x}, \frac{1}{1+\ln y}, \frac{1}{1+\ln z}$ are in

questions.question: If S_n = 4 + 11 + 21 + 34 + 50 +... to n terms, then $\frac{1}{60}$(s_29 - s_9) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If Sn = 4 + 11 + 21 + 34 + 50 +... to n terms, then {tex}\frac{1}{60}{/tex}(s29 - s9) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Sn = 4 + 11 + 21 + 34 + 50 +... + n terms.
Difference are in A.P.
Let Tn = an2 + bn + c
T1 = a + b + c = 4 ...(i)
T2 = 4a + 2b + c = 11 ...(ii)
T3 = 9a + 3b + c = 21 ...(iii)
By solving equations (i), (ii) & (iii), we get
{tex}\mathrm{a}=\frac{3}{2}, \mathrm{~b}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}, c = 0
So, Tn = {tex}\frac{3}{2} n^2+\frac{5}{2} n{/tex}
Sn = {tex}\sum \mathrm{T} _{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{3}{2} \sum \mathrm{n}^2+\frac{5}{2} \sum \mathrm{n}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6}+\frac{5}{2} \frac{(\mathrm{n})(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{n(n+1)}{4}[2 n+1+5]{/tex}
{tex}S_n=\frac{n(n+1)}{4}(2 n+6){/tex} {tex}=\frac{n(n+1)(n+3)}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{60}\left(\frac{29 \times 30 \times 32}{2}-\frac{9 \times 10 \times 12}{2}\right)=223{/tex}

a40 = {tex}\frac{3(40)^2-3(40)+10}{2}=2345{/tex},

questions.question: Let $ \left\{a_n\right\}_{n=0}^{\infty} $ be a sequence such that a_0 = a_1 = 0 and $ a_{n+2}=3 a_{n+1}-2 a_{n+1}, $ $\forall n \geq 0 $. Then a_25a_23 - 2a_25a_22 - 2a_23a_24 + 4a_22a_24 is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex} \left\{a_n\right\}_{n=0}^{\infty} {/tex} be a sequence such that a0 = a1 = 0 and {tex} a_{n+2}=3 a_{n+1}-2 a_{n+1}, {/tex} {tex}\forall n \geq 0 {/tex}. Then a25a23 - 2a25a22 - 2a23a24 + 4a22a24 is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Given relation is a0 = 0, a1= 0 and an + 2
= 3an + 1 - 2an + 1 : n {tex}\geq{/tex} 0
an + 2 - an + 1 = 2(an + 1 - an) + 1
Put n = 0, a2 - a1 = 2(a1 - a0) + 1
Put n = 1, a3 - a2 = 2(a2 - a1) + 1
Put n = 2, a4 - a3 = 2(a3 - a2) + 1
Put n = n, an + 2 - an + 1 = 2(an + 1 - an) + 1
(an + 2 - a1) - 2(an + 1 - a0) - (n + 1) = 0
an + 2 = 2an + 1 + (n + 1)
n {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} n - 2
an - 2an - 1 = n - 1 
Now a25a23 - 2a25 a22 - 2a22a24 + 4a22a24
= (a25 - 2a24)(a23 - 2a22) = (24)(22) = 528

questions.question: Consider the sequence a_1, a_2, a_3,... such that a_1 = 1, a_2 = 2 and a_n + 2 = $\frac{2}{a_{n+1}} + a_n$ for n = 1, 2, 3,... If $\left(\frac{a_1+\frac{1}{a_2}}{a_3}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{a_2+\frac{1}{a_3}}{a_4}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{a_3+\frac{1}{a_4}}{a_5}\right) $ $\cdots\left(\frac{a_{30}+\frac{1}{a_{31}}}{a_{32}}\right)=2^\alpha\left({ }^{61} C_{31}\right), $ then $\alpha$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: Consider the sequence a1, a2, a3,... such that a1 = 1, a2 = 2 and  an + 2 = {tex}\frac{2}{a_{n+1}}  + a_n{/tex} for n = 1, 2, 3,... If {tex}\left(\frac{a_1+\frac{1}{a_2}}{a_3}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{a_2+\frac{1}{a_3}}{a_4}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{a_3+\frac{1}{a_4}}{a_5}\right) {/tex} {tex}\cdots\left(\frac{a_{30}+\frac{1}{a_{31}}}{a_{32}}\right)=2^\alpha\left({ }^{61} C_{31}\right), {/tex} then {tex}\alpha{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Now from given eqn. an+2 = {tex}\frac{2}{a_{n+1}}+a_n {/tex}
an+2 an+1 - an+1 an = 2
Here series will satisfy
a1a2, a2a3, a3a4, a4a5,... 
= 1.2, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4,...
Now {tex} \frac{a_n+\frac{1}{a_{n+1}}}{a_{n+2}}=\frac{a_{n+2}-\frac{1}{a_{n+1}}}{a_{n+2}} {/tex}
= {tex}1-\frac{1}{a_{n+1} a_{n+2}}=1-\frac{1}{2(r+1)}=\frac{2 r+1}{2(r+1)} {/tex}
Here proof is given by
{tex} =\prod \limits_{r=1}^{30} \frac{(2 r+1)}{2(r+1)} {/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot \ldots 61)}{2^{30} \cdot(2 \cdot 3 \cdot \ldots \ 31)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot \ldots \ldots \ldots .61)}{ 31! \cdot 2^{30}} \times \frac{2^{30} \times {30!}}{2^{30} \times {30!}} {/tex}
{tex}=\frac{ 61!}{2^{60} {31!} \cdot 30!} \Rightarrow \alpha=-60{/tex}

questions.question: The sum of the infinite series $1+\frac{5}{6}+\frac{12}{6^2}+\frac{22}{6^3}+\frac{35}{6^4}+\frac{51}{6^5}+\frac{70}{6^6}+\ldots $ is equal to:

{tex}\frac{S}{6}=\frac{1}{6}+\frac{5}{6^2}+\frac{12}{6^3}+\frac{22}{6^4}+\ldots{/tex} ...(ii)
On subtracting (i) from (ii)
{tex}\frac{5}{6} S=1+\frac{4}{6}+\frac{7}{6^2}+\frac{10}{6^3}+\frac{13}{6^4}+\cdots{/tex}
{tex}\frac{5}{36} S=\frac{1}{6}+\frac{4}{6^2}+\frac{7}{6^3}+\frac{10}{6^4}+\frac{13}{6^5}+\cdots{/tex}
on subtraction 
{tex}\frac{25}{36} S=1+\frac{3}{6}+\frac{3}{6^2}+\frac{3}{6^3}+\ldots{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{5} {/tex}; {tex} S=\frac{8}{5} \times \frac{36}{25}=\frac{288}{125}{/tex}

questions.question: Let S = 2 + $\frac{6}{7}+\frac{12}{7^2}+\frac{20}{7^3}+\frac{30}{7^4}+\ldots $ then 4S is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let S = 2 + {tex}\frac{6}{7}+\frac{12}{7^2}+\frac{20}{7^3}+\frac{30}{7^4}+\ldots {/tex} then 4S is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given series is S = 2{tex}+\frac{6}{7}+\frac{12}{7^2}+\frac{20}{7^3}+\frac{30}{7^4}+\ldots {/tex}
Multiply both sides by {tex}\frac 17{/tex}, then subtract from 5.

Again, multiply both sides by {tex}\frac 17{/tex} & subtract.
{tex}\frac{\Rightarrow \frac{6 S}{7^2}=\frac{2}{7}+\frac{4}{7^2}+\frac{6}{7^3}+\frac{8}{7^4}+\ldots}{\frac{6 S}{7}\left(1-\frac{1}{7}\right)=2+\frac{2}{7}+\frac{2}{7^2}+\frac{2}{7^3}+\ldots}{/tex}
Apply, {tex}S_{\infty}=\frac{a}{1-r}{/tex} with a = 2 & r = {tex}\frac 17{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{6^2 S}{7^2}=\frac{2}{1-\frac{1}{7}}=\frac{2}{6} \times 7{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow S=\frac{2 \times 7^3}{6^3} \Rightarrow 4 S=\frac{7^3}{3^3}=\left(\frac{7}{3}\right)^3{/tex}

questions.question: The number of common terms in the progressions $4,9,14,19, \ldots \ldots$. up to $25^{\text {th }}$ term and $3,6,9,12, \ldots \ldots$., up to $37^{\text {th }}$ term is:
questions.question_raw: The number of common terms in the progressions {tex}4,9,14,19, \ldots \ldots{/tex}. up to {tex}25^{\text {th }}{/tex} term and {tex}3,6,9,12, \ldots \ldots{/tex}., up to {tex}37^{\text {th }}{/tex} term is:
questions.explanation:
First A.P., {tex}4,9,14,19, \ldots ., 48{/tex} to {tex}25^{\text {th }}{/tex} term
Here, {tex}a=4, d=5{/tex}
So, {tex}a_{25}=4+24(5)=124{/tex}
Second A.P., {tex}3,6,9,12, \ldots . .48{/tex} to {tex}37^{\text {th }}{/tex} term
Here, {tex}a=3 d=3{/tex}
So, {tex}a_{37}=3+36(3)=111{/tex}
As, LCM {tex}(5,3){/tex} = 15
So, we have, {tex}9+(x-1) 15 \leq 111{/tex} ({tex}\because 9{/tex} is the first common term)
{tex}\Rightarrow 9+15 n-15 \leq 111{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 15 n-6 \Rightarrow 111 \Rightarrow 15 n \leq 117{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow n \leq 7.4{/tex}. So, {tex}n=7{/tex}

questions.question: If $\sum_\limits{r=1}^{n} T_{r}=\frac{(2 n-1)(2 n+1)(2 n+3)(2 n+5)}{64}$, then $\lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{r=1}^{n}\left(\frac{1}{T_{r}}\right)$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\sum_\limits{r=1}^{n} T_{r}=\frac{(2 n-1)(2 n+1)(2 n+3)(2 n+5)}{64}{/tex}, then {tex}\lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{r=1}^{n}\left(\frac{1}{T_{r}}\right){/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\sum_{r=1}^{n} T_{r}=\frac{(2 n-1)(2 n+1)(2 n+3)(2 n+5)}{64}{/tex}
{tex}T_{n}=S_{n}-S_{n-1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(2 n-1)(2 n+1)(2 n+3)(2 n+5)}{64}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-(2 n-3)(2 n-1)(2 n+1)(2 n+3)}{64}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(2 n-1)(2 n+1)(2 n+3)(2 n+5-2 n+3)}{64}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(2 n-1)(2 n+1)(2 n+3)}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\because \sum_\limits{r=1}^{n} \frac{1}{T_{r}}=8 \sum_\limits{r=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(2 n-1)(2 n+1)(2 n+3)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8}{4}\left(\sum_{r=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(2 n-1)(2 n+1)}-\frac{1}{(2 n+1)(2 n+3)}\right){/tex}
{tex}=2\left[\begin{array}{l}\left(\frac{1}{1 \cdot 3}-\frac{1}{3 \cdot 5}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{3 \cdot 5}-\frac{1}{5 \cdot 7}\right) \ldots \\ \cdots\left(\frac{1}{(2 n-1)(2 n+1)}-\frac{1}{(2 n+1)(2 n+3)}\right)\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=2\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{(2 n+1)(2 n+3)}\right){/tex}
At {tex}n \rightarrow \infty{/tex} required value {tex}=2 \times \frac{1}{3}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: Let $a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \ldots$ be a GP of increasing positive terms. If $a_{1} a_{5}=28$ and $a_{2}+a_{4}=29$, then $a_{6}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \ldots{/tex} be a GP of increasing positive terms. If {tex}a_{1} a_{5}=28{/tex} and {tex}a_{2}+a_{4}=29{/tex}, then {tex}a_{6}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots{/tex} GP
{tex}a_{1} a_{5}=28{/tex} and {tex}a_{2}+a_{4}=29{/tex}
Let {tex}a{/tex} be the first term and '{tex}r{/tex}' be the common ratio
{tex}a\left(a r^{4}\right)=28{/tex} and {tex}a r+a r^{3}=29{/tex}
{tex}a^{2} r^{4}=28{/tex}
{tex}a r^{2}=2 \sqrt{7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \sqrt{7}}{r}+2 \sqrt{7} r=29{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{7} r^{2}-29 r+2 \sqrt{7}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{7} r^{2}-28 r-r+2 \sqrt{7}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{7} r(r-2 \sqrt{7}){/tex} {tex}-1(r-2 \sqrt{7})=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(r-2 \sqrt{7})(2 \sqrt{7} r-1)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow r=2 \sqrt{7}, r=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{7}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Increasing GP {tex}\Rightarrow r=2 \sqrt{7}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a=\frac{2}{2 \sqrt{7}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a_{6}=a r^{5}=(2 \sqrt{7})^{4}{/tex} {tex}=(28)^{2}=784{/tex}
